content:
# Joshua Chapter 17 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), folio pages 240-241
**Source Image:** `JOS_017_page_001.jpg`, `JOS_017_page_002.jpg`

---

**17:1** And the lot was for the tribe of Manasseh, for he was the firstborn of Joseph. As for Machir the firstborn of Manasseh, the father of Gilead — because he was a man of war — Gilead and Bashan were his.

**17:2** And there was a lot for the remaining sons of Manasseh according to their clans: for the sons of Abiezer, and for the sons of Helek, and for the sons of Asriel, and for the sons of Shechem, and for the sons of Hepher, and for the sons of Shemida. These were the male sons of Manasseh son of Joseph, according to their clans.

**17:3** But Zelophehad son of Hepher, son of Gilead, son of Machir, son of Manasseh, had no sons but only daughters. And these are the names of his daughters: Mahlah, and Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.

**17:4** And they drew near before Eleazar the priest, and before Joshua son of Nun, and before the leaders, saying, "YHWH commanded Moses to give us an inheritance among our brothers." And he gave them, according to the mouth of YHWH, an inheritance among the brothers of their father.

**17:5** And ten portions fell to Manasseh, besides the land of Gilead and Bashan, which is beyond the Jordan,

**17:6** because the daughters of Manasseh received an inheritance among his sons. And the land of Gilead belonged to the remaining sons of Manasseh.

**17:7** And the border of Manasseh was from Asher to Michmethath, which is before Shechem. And the border went to the right, to the inhabitants of En-tappuah.

**17:8** The land of Tappuah belonged to Manasseh, but Tappuah on the border of Manasseh belonged to the sons of Ephraim.

**17:9** And the border went down to the Wadi of Kanah, southward of the wadi. These cities belonged to Ephraim in the midst of the cities of Manasseh. And the border of Manasseh was on the north side of the wadi, and its outlet was at the sea.

**17:10** To the south it was Ephraim's, and to the north it was Manasseh's, and the sea was its border. And on Asher they met on the north, and on Issachar on the east.

**17:11** And Manasseh had in Issachar and in Asher: Beth-shean and its daughter-towns, and Ibleam and its daughter-towns, and the inhabitants of Dor and its daughter-towns, and the inhabitants of En-dor and its daughter-towns, and the inhabitants of Taanach and its daughter-towns, and the inhabitants of Megiddo and its daughter-towns — the three of the heights.

**17:12** But the sons of Manasseh were not able to dispossess these cities, and the Canaanites were determined to dwell in this land.

**17:13** And it was, when the Sons of Israel grew strong, that they put the Canaanites to forced labor, but dispossessing, they did not dispossess them.

**17:14** And the sons of Joseph spoke to Joshua, saying, "Why have you given me but one lot and one portion as an inheritance, when I am a numerous people, since YHWH has blessed me until now?"

**17:15** And Joshua said to them, "If you are a numerous people, go up to the forest and clear a place for yourself there in the land of the Perizzites and the Rephaim, since the hill country of Ephraim is too narrow for you."

**17:16** And the sons of Joseph said, "The hill country is not enough for us, and all the Canaanites who dwell in the land of the valley have chariots of iron — both those in Beth-shean and its daughter-towns, and those in the Valley of Jezreel."

**17:17** And Joshua said to the house of Joseph — to Ephraim and to Manasseh — saying, "You are a numerous people, and great is your strength. You shall not have one lot only,

**17:18** but the hill country shall be yours. Though it is a forest, you shall clear it, and its farthest extents shall be yours. For you shall dispossess the Canaanites, though they have chariots of iron and though they are strong."

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Method
Each verse was compared across all three translations (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) and checked against the manuscript images of Leningrad Codex pages 240-241. Where all three translators agreed on a reading, that agreement was adopted. Where they diverged, the manuscript was consulted and the wisest synthesis was chosen, favoring accuracy to the Hebrew while maintaining readable English. No Codex Vaticanus (LXX) images were available for this chapter; LXX observations are drawn from the translators' notes.

### Agreements
The three translators demonstrated strong agreement across Joshua 17. All three preserve:
- **YHWH** (not "the LORD") throughout (vv. 4 twice, 14)
- **Sons of Israel** (not "Israelites") in v. 13
- **"man of war"** for ish milchamah in v. 1
- **Zelophehad's five daughters** named in the same order as Numbers 26:33
- **"ten portions"** in v. 5 with the same derivation (five male clans + five daughters)
- **"forced labor"** for mas in v. 13
- **"chariots of iron"** for rekhev barzel in vv. 16, 18
- **"Rephaim"** retained as a proper noun in v. 15
- **"Wadi of Kanah"** for nachal qanah in v. 9
- The essential structure of Joshua's two-part response (vv. 15, 17-18) as challenge rather than consolation

### Major Decisions

**1. Proper name forms: anglicized vs. transliterated (throughout)**
EMET uses Hebrew transliterations (Menasheh, Yoseph, Yehoshua, Yarden, Ephrayim, Yissakhar, Yizreel, etc.), while LOGOS and PNEUMA use anglicized forms (Manasseh, Joseph, Joshua, Jordan, Ephraim, Issachar, Jezreel). SOPHIA adopts anglicized forms, consistent with the established convention for universally recognized English equivalents. See Disagreement Log.

**2. "Drew near" vs. "came near" vs. "came before" (v. 4)**
EMET renders "drew near" (vatiqravna), LOGOS renders "came near," and PNEUMA renders "came before." SOPHIA adopts "drew near" (EMET), which most precisely renders the Hebrew qarav (to draw near, approach) and preserves its formal, ritual connotation — the daughters are approaching a judicial assembly. See Disagreement Log.

**3. "According to the mouth of YHWH" vs. "in keeping with YHWH's command" (v. 4)**
EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew idiom "according to the mouth of YHWH" (el pi YHWH), while PNEUMA renders "in keeping with YHWH's command." SOPHIA preserves the literal idiom — the "mouth" metaphor conveys that the inheritance was granted by YHWH's own spoken word to Moses (Num 27:6-7), not merely a general decree. See Disagreement Log.

**4. "Among our brothers" vs. "among our kinsmen" (v. 4)**
EMET and LOGOS render "among our brothers" (betokh acheinu), while PNEUMA renders "among our kinsmen." SOPHIA adopts "among our brothers" — ach means "brother" and the literal rendering preserves the daughters' claim to full fraternal standing within the tribe. See Disagreement Log.

**5. "Inherited an inheritance" vs. "received an inheritance" (v. 6)**
EMET renders "inherited an inheritance" (preserving the cognate accusative nachalah...nachal), LOGOS and PNEUMA render "received an inheritance." SOPHIA adopts "received an inheritance" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) because the English cognate construction "inherited an inheritance" is awkward, and "received" accurately conveys the Qal of nachal in this context. See Disagreement Log.

**6. "Upon the face of Shechem" vs. "before Shechem" vs. "which faces Shechem" (v. 7)**
EMET renders "upon the face of Shechem" (al penei Shekhem), LOGOS renders "which is before Shechem," PNEUMA renders "which faces Shechem." SOPHIA adopts "which is before Shechem" (LOGOS), balancing the Hebrew spatial idiom with natural English while preserving the directional sense. See Disagreement Log.

**7. "To the right" vs. "southward" (v. 7)**
EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew "to the right" (ha-yamin), while PNEUMA renders "southward" (the directional meaning of yamin when facing east). SOPHIA retains "to the right" (EMET/LOGOS), preserving the Hebrew directional idiom. See Disagreement Log.

**8. "Daughter-towns" vs. "daughters" vs. "surrounding towns" (vv. 11, 16)**
EMET renders "its daughters" (literal), LOGOS renders "its daughter-towns," PNEUMA renders "its surrounding towns." SOPHIA adopts "daughter-towns" (LOGOS) — it preserves the Hebrew kinship metaphor (benoteha, "her daughters") while making the meaning immediately clear. See Disagreement Log.

**9. "The three of the heights" vs. "three of the height" vs. "the three of Naphath" (v. 11)**
EMET renders "three of the height," LOGOS renders "the three of the heights," PNEUMA renders "the three of Naphath." The Hebrew sheloshet hannafet is notoriously difficult. SOPHIA adopts "the three of the heights" (LOGOS), which preserves the Hebrew structure and the most likely meaning of nafet as "heights" or "elevated districts." PNEUMA's retention of the Hebrew term "Naphath" is defensible but less informative. See Disagreement Log.

**10. "Was determined" vs. "persisted" vs. "were determined" (v. 12)**
EMET renders "was determined" (singular, matching the collective "the Canaanite"), LOGOS renders "persisted," PNEUMA renders "were determined." The Hebrew ho'il (Hiphil of ya'al) conveys willful resolve. SOPHIA adopts "were determined" with the plural "Canaanites" (LOGOS/PNEUMA), as the English plural reads more naturally, combined with "determined" which best captures the Hebrew sense of willful insistence. See Disagreement Log.

**11. Infinitive absolute: "dispossessing, they did not dispossess" vs. "utterly dispossess" vs. "drive out completely" (v. 13)**
EMET renders "dispossessing they did not dispossess him" (horesh lo horisho), LOGOS renders "they did not utterly dispossess them," PNEUMA renders "they did not drive them out completely." The Hebrew infinitive absolute construction (horesh lo horish) intensifies the negation. SOPHIA adopts a hybrid: "dispossessing, they did not dispossess them" — preserving the infinitive absolute doubling per project convention while using the plural "them" for natural English. See Disagreement Log.

**12. "A great people" vs. "a numerous people" (vv. 14-15, 17)**
EMET renders "a great people" (am rav), LOGOS and PNEUMA render "a numerous people." SOPHIA adopts "a numerous people" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — the Hebrew rav in this context emphasizes quantity (population size), not quality (greatness), and "numerous" captures that emphasis. See Disagreement Log.

**13. "Too narrow" vs. "too cramped" (v. 15)**
EMET and LOGOS render "too narrow" (ats), PNEUMA renders "too cramped." SOPHIA adopts "too narrow" (EMET/LOGOS, 2-1), which directly renders the Hebrew ats. See Disagreement Log.

**14. "Is not found sufficient" vs. "is not enough" (v. 16)**
EMET renders "is not found [sufficient]" (lo yimmatse'), LOGOS renders "is not enough," PNEUMA renders "is not enough." SOPHIA adopts "is not enough" (LOGOS/PNEUMA, 2-1) — the Hebrew idiom "will not be found" means "is insufficient," and "is not enough" conveys this clearly. See Disagreement Log.

**15. "Its goings out" vs. "its farthest extents" vs. "its farthest reaches" (v. 18)**
EMET renders "its goings out" (totse'otav), LOGOS renders "its farthest extents," PNEUMA renders "its farthest reaches." SOPHIA adopts "its farthest extents" (LOGOS), which conveys the meaning of totse'ot (lit. "goings-out," i.e., outermost boundaries) without the awkwardness of the literal rendering. See Disagreement Log.

**16. Narrative connector "And it was" (v. 13)**
EMET and LOGOS preserve the wayehi construction ("And it was, when..."), while PNEUMA drops it ("When the Sons of Israel grew stronger..."). SOPHIA preserves it, consistent with the project convention of retaining Hebrew narrative architecture. See Disagreement Log.

**17. "Great is your strength" vs. "you have great power" vs. "with great strength" (v. 17)**
EMET renders "great is your strength" (koach gadol), LOGOS renders "you have great power," PNEUMA renders "with great strength." SOPHIA adopts "great is your strength" (EMET), which mirrors the Hebrew predicate-first word order and creates a parallel with "a numerous people" — both are declarative statements by Joshua affirming the tribe's capacity. See Disagreement Log.

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex)
The manuscript images (pages 240-241) confirm the standard MT text of Joshua 17 as reflected in all three translations. The three-column layout is clearly legible. Notable observations:
- The verse divisions and cantillation marks are consistent with the BHS critical apparatus.
- The spelling of sheloshet hannafet (v. 11) in the manuscript supports the reading as a common noun ("heights") rather than a proper noun, though the ambiguity is genuine.
- The infinitive absolute horesh in v. 13 is clearly written before the finite verb horisho, confirming the emphatic construction preserved in the SOPHIA text.

---
